content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 21, 1991


Stella Levy, Tax Counsel
State Board of Equalization
1020 N Street
Sacramento, CA  94279-0001

Re:  Your Request or Advice
     Our File No. A-91-434

Dear Ms. Levy:

     You have requested advice concerning the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/  The State Board of
Equalization is in the final stages of approving amendments to its
conflict of interest code.  A few questions have arisen as a
result of that amendment process.

QUESTIONS

     1.  Are state agency conflict of interest codes subject to
review by the Office of Administrative Law?

     2.  Is the inclusion of classifications in a code subject to
state collective bargaining agreements?

CONCLUSIONS

     1.  The adoption or amendment of conflict of interest codes
are not subject to review by the Office of Administrative Law.

     2.  The inclusion of classifications into codes are not
subject to state collective bargaining agreements.


_________________________

1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento CA 95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

October 21, 1991
Page Two


ANALYSIS

In 1976, the Commission adopted Regulation 2 Cal. Code of
Regs. Section 18750 which sets out the procedures that state
agencies must follow to adopt, or amend, a conflict of interest
code. The regulation provides that a notice of the proposed code,
or amendment, must be prepared and filed with the Office of
Administrative Law for publication in the California Notice
Register. It provides that at the expiration of the 45-day
comment period, the agency will forward the proposed code, or
amendments, along with specific supporting documentation for
review by the Fair Political Practices Commission.2/ Once the
Commission has approved the code, the agency shall transmit it to
the Office of Administrative Law, which shall file the code or
amendment promptly with the Secretary of State without further
review pursuant to Article 6 of Title 2 of the Government Code.
(Regulation 18750(k).)

When your code is submitted to the Office of Administrative
Law, a rulemaking file is not required to accompany it. The
Office of Administrative Law will merely file the proposed code
with the Office of the Secretary of State without conducting the
same review it conducts on rules or regulations.

With regard to including a position in a code only if its
inclusion has been negotiated as part of a union collective
bargaining agreement, there is nothing in the Political Reform Act
that specifies that inclusion of positions in a code must first be
negotiated through the unions. A position is designated3/ in a
code if it is involved in the making or participation in the
making of decisions which may have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on any economic interest. (Section
87302.) The inclusion of a position in a conflict of interest

2/ The Fair Political Practices Commission is the code reviewing
body for state agency conflict of interest codes. (Section
82011.) No conflict of interest code shall be effective until it
has been approved by the code reviewing body. (Section 87303.)

3/ A "designated position" means any officer, employee, member, or
consultant of any agency whose position with the agency is
designated in a code because the position entails the making or
participation in the making of decision which may foreseeably have
a material effect on any financial interest or is involved as a
state employee at other than a clerical or ministerial level in
the functions of negotiating or signing any contract awarded
through competitive bidding, or making decisions in conjunction
with the competitive bidding process, or in negotiating, signing,
or making decisions on contracts executed pursuant to Section
10122 of the Public Contract Code. (Section 82019.)

October 21, 1991
Page Three


code is determined by the responsibilities assigned to the
position. If the responsibilities dictate that the position be
included, exclusion of such position would violate the express
provisions of the Act as well as the intent and purposes expressed
in Section 81004(c).

I trust this answers your concerns. If I can assist you
further, please feel free to call me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[illegible signature]

By: Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division

SH/JET/jt

sections.question:
1. Are state agency conflict of interest codes subject to review by the Office of Administrative Law?

2. Is the inclusion of classifications in a code subject to state collective bargaining agreements?
sections.conclusion:
1. The adoption or amendment of conflict of interest codes are not subject to review by the Office of Administrative Law.

2. The inclusion of classifications into codes are not subject to state collective bargaining agreements.

1, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

In 1976, the Commission adopted Regulation 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18750 which sets out the procedures that state agencies must follow to adopt, or amend, a conflict of interest code. The regulation provides that a notice of the proposed code, or amendment, must be prepared and filed with the Office of Administrative Law for publication in the California Notice Register. It provides that at the expiration of the 45-day comment period, the agency will forward the proposed code, or amendments, along with specific supporting documentation for review by the Fair Political Practices Commission.2/ Once the Commission has approved the code, the agency shall transmit it to the Office of Administrative Law, which shall file the code or amendment promptly with the Secretary of State without further review pursuant to Article 6 of Title 2 of the Government Code. (Regulation 18750(k).)

When your code is submitted to the Office of Administrative Law, a rulemaking file is not required to accompany it. The Office of Administrative Law will merely file the proposed code with the Office of the Secretary of State without conducting the same review it conducts on rules or regulations.

With regard to including a position in a code only if its inclusion has been negotiated as part of a union collective bargaining agreement, there is nothing in the Political Reform Act that specifies that inclusion of positions in a code must first be negotiated through the unions. A position is designated3/ in a code if it is involved in the making or participation in the making of decisions which may have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any economic interest. (Section 87302.) The inclusion of a position in a conflict of interest

2/ The Fair Political Practices Commission is the code reviewing body for state agency conflict of interest codes. (Section 82011.) No conflict of interest code shall be effective until it has been approved by the code reviewing body. (Section 87303.)

3/ A "designated position" means any officer, employee, member, or consultant of any agency whose position with the agency is designated in a code because the position entails the making or participation in the making of decision which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest or is involved as a state employee at other than a clerical or ministerial level in the functions of negotiating or signing any contract awarded through competitive bidding, or making decisions in conjunction with the competitive bidding process, or in negotiating, signing, or making decisions on contracts executed pursuant to Section 10122 of the Public Contract Code. 2. Is the inclusion of classifications in a code subject to state collective bargaining agreements?

2. The inclusion of classifications into codes are not subject to state collective bargaining agreements.

1, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
